items.q: How do insulin and glucagon work together to regulate blood glucose?
items.correct: They are antagonistic — insulin lowers blood glucose (cells take up glucose, store as glycogen); glucagon raises it (glycogen broken back down to glucose).
items.misconception: Insulin and glucagon both lower blood glucose using different mechanisms.
items.random_wrong: Insulin works on muscles and glucagon works on the brain.
items.why: Students don't grasp 'antagonistic hormones' — they often think both hormones do similar jobs because both are about glucose.

items.q: What makes antibodies effective against pathogens?
items.correct: They have a specific shape complementary to antigens on a particular pathogen, marking it for destruction or neutralising it.
items.misconception: Antibodies are general-purpose proteins that work against any pathogen.
items.random_wrong: Antibodies are tiny knives that physically cut pathogens apart.
items.why: Students don't grasp antibody specificity, often picturing them as generic 'germ killers' rather than precision tools matching one antigen.

items.q: What does insulin do to blood glucose?
items.correct: It lowers blood glucose by causing cells (especially liver and muscle) to take it up and store it as glycogen.
items.misconception: Insulin raises blood glucose to give the body energy.
items.random_wrong: Insulin destroys glucose in the bloodstream so it cannot be used.
items.why: Students associate sugar with energy and 'doing something with sugar' with raising energy, missing that insulin removes glucose from blood.

items.q: Which enzyme unwinds the DNA double helix?
items.why: It's Helicase. “Ligase” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: AUG codes for which amino acid (and serves as start)?
items.why: It's Methionine. “Tryptophan” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A gene has 900 base pairs in its coding region (no introns). How many amino acids does it encode?
items.why: The real figure is 300. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
